chunk_summary: Caregiver teach-back ensures safe feeding practices for children with neurodisability, especially during intercurrent illness, emphasizing IDDSI levels, bolus size, pacing, and emergency triggers.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of caregiver teach-back in feeding support for neurodisability.
Learn to identify stop signs and escalation criteria during feeding.
Recognize safe feeding modifications during intercurrent illness.
Apply IDDSI testing methods for thickened fluids and bolus preparation.
key_facts: Teach-back requires caregivers to demonstrate skills and explain stop criteria before discharge or plan changes.
Minimum competencies include correct positioning, IDDSI level preparation, bolus size, pacing, and oral hygiene management.
Stop signs include wet voice, persistent cough, distress, drowsiness, and increased work of breathing.
During intercurrent illness, simplify feeding to the safest IDDSI level, reduce bolus volumes, and shift hydration to tube route if coughing with sips begins.
Emergency triggers include rising respiratory rate, oxygen saturation decline, and inability to maintain hydration.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Teach-back: a systematic review of implementation and impacts — 'Teach-back improves caregiver understanding and adherence to complex care plans, reducing errors and improving patient outcomes.'
document_key_topics: IDDSI testing methods and bolus preparation
Emergency care triggers in neurodisability feeding
